channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: The Hidden Dead: The True Toll in Venezuela Is Buried Under Rubble
item.description: Five days after devastating twin earthquakes flattened entire residential neighborhoods, experts fear the official death toll of 1,719 could be a serious undercount.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola, Leo Sands, Isayen Herrera and Tibisay Romero
item.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
item.media:description: The authorities have started an improvised morgue, so that relatives can look for the bodies of loved ones among the earthquake’s victims at La Guaira’s port in Venezuela.

item.title: U.S. and Iran to Meet with Mediators in Qatar
item.description: American and Iranian officials are in the Gulf state, a key intermediary between the two countries, days after new round of attacks threatened efforts to sign a lasting peace deal.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Vahid Salemi/Associated Press

item.title: The Prom Went On in Kyiv, but Masha’s Date Danced Alone
item.description: Masha Polska, 15, was an avid dancer who had been dreaming of a star turn in the group waltz. That was not to be.
item.dc:creator: Siobhán O’Grady, Liubov Sholudko and Oksana Parafeniuk
item.media:description: Yehor Holodryha, center, and classmates of Masha Polska dancing the waltz at their prom in Kyiv, Ukraine, in June.

item.title: After U.S.-Iran War, Oman Is Said to Propose Strait of Hormuz Fee Plan
item.description: Under the proposal, Iran and Oman would collect payment for ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz, according to officials and diplomats with knowledge of the matter.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim, Farnaz Fassihi and Erika Solomon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Elke Scholiers/Getty Images
item.media:description: A cargo vessel anchored off the coast of Oman near Muscat last week.

item.title: Shortages of Rescue Equipment Hampered Venezuela’s Earthquake Response
item.description: Residents appealed for excavators, generators and specialized rescue tools as civilians have stepped in to supply crews working through the rubble.
item.dc:creator: Genevieve Glatsky and Tibisay Romero
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: A collapsed building in Catia La Mar, La Guaira state, Venezuela.

item.title: World Cup Loss Dominates German News, Displacing Even a Mass Shooting
item.description: A day after a deadly attack in northern Germany, the news media’s attention largely shifted to the country’s poor performance at the World Cup.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.media:credit: Ibrahim Ot/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Forensic police officers on Tuesday at the site of a shooting in Stade, Germany.

item.title: Parcel Bomb Explodes in Monaco, Injuring 3
item.description: The blast seriously injured three people, including a child, according to the authorities, who said the assailant was on the run.
item.dc:creator: Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:credit: Valery Hache/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: An apartment building in Monaco where an explosion occurred on Tuesday.

item.title: Australian Man Charged With Homicide After Thai Teen’s Body Found in Suitcase
item.description: The 17-year-old’s remains were found abandoned near railroad tracks in Pattaya. The suspect was arrested while trying to leave the country.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Kim and Muktita Suhartono
item.media:credit: Teerasak Suttativong/Reuters
item.media:description: Rescue workers, police officers and forensic technicians searched an area near railway tracks on Saturday, before finding a teenager’s body, in Pattaya, Thailand.

item.title: Norway’s ‘Viking Row’ Is a Fan Phenomenon at the World Cup
item.description: Seas of red-clad Norwegian supporters are rowing shoulder to shoulder at stadiums, schools, even a nursing home. Not everyone is thrilled.
item.dc:creator: Louise Krüger and Lynsey Chutel
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban for The New York Times
item.media:description: Norway fans at a World Cup group stage match against Senegal in East Rutherford, N.J., on June 22.

item.title: What Italy’s Failure to Make the Soccer World Cup Says About Its Wider Woes
item.description: For some Italians, their team’s absence from the biggest tournament in soccer is a metaphor for a broader national malaise.
item.dc:creator: Motoko Rich
item.media:credit: Alessandro Grassani for The New York Times
item.media:description: At Campo de’ Fiori Square in Rome, restaurants and cafes set up televisions showing a World Cup match on June 17.

item.title: More Than 1 Million Migrants Apply for Legal Status in Spain
item.description: Since late April, the Spanish government has allowed undocumented migrants a path out of legal limbo, making Spain a beacon to the global left.
item.dc:creator: Carlos Barragán
item.media:credit: Violeta Santos Moura/Reuters
item.media:description: Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez of Spain presenting the country’s citizenship and integration plan in Madrid on Tuesday.

item.title: China Increases Military and Economic Pressure on Japan
item.description: Beijing’s moves have included flying bombers near Japan, detaining businesspeople and restricting the exports of rare earths.
item.dc:creator: Lily Kuo, Pei-Lin Wu and Kiuko Notoya
item.media:credit: CFOTO/Future Publishing via Getty Images
item.media:description: A Chinese coast guard ship departing from the dock in Yantai for a patrol in April.

item.title: Trump Cut a Billion-Dollar Mining Deal. His Sons Stand to Profit.
item.description: An agreement between the U.S. and Kazakhstan has given a group of American investors with ties to the president and the commerce secretary access to one of the world’s largest untapped reserves of tungsten.
item.dc:creator: Paul Sonne and Eric Lipton
item.media:credit: Sergey Ponomarev for The New York Times

item.title: Pakistan Attacks Afghanistan, Killing More Than 30
item.description: The Taliban said that airstrikes left 36 civilians dead, while Pakistan claimed that its assault killed 32 militants, the latest in a monthslong conflict.
item.dc:creator: Elian Peltier and Safiullah Padshah
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Onlookers at the site of a Pakistani airstrike in Paktia Province, Afghanistan on Monday.

item.title: Turkey as a Tango Destination? A Reporter Was Living That Story.
item.description: A Times journalist profiled Istanbul’s thriving tango scene after finding herself transformed by the dance.
item.dc:creator: Safak Timur
item.media:credit: Laura Boushnak for The New York Times
item.media:description: Safak Timur, a reporter in The Times’s Istanbul bureau, dancing at a tango school in Istanbul. Aficionados say they can identify Turks by their dancing.

item.title: Archaeologists Find Maya Monuments Off the Beaten Path. Way Off.
item.description: Scientists drove ATVs for miles and then hiked miles more through dense forest to reach a site untouched by looting on Mexico’s Yucatán Peninsula. They named it Minanbé, meaning “there is no path.”
item.dc:creator: Alan Yuhas and Emiliano Rodríguez Mega

item.title: Driver Is Reported Dead Days After Plowing Into World Cup Fans in Mexico
item.description: The episode appears to the most significant violence after wins by the Mexico national team, which has drawn huge crowds onto streets across the country.
item.dc:creator: Mack Liederman
item.media:credit: Ayuntamiento De Los Cabos/Facebook, via Reuters
item.media:description: Security personnel and emergency responders at the site where a car rammed through a crowd of people in Cabo San Lucas, Mexico, last week.

item.title: Beaver Statue Honoring the United States Is Smashed in Toronto
item.description: One of dozens of statues of Canada’s national animal, part of a public art installation for the World Cup, was destroyed. It was the one wearing stars and stripes.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Old Town Toronto Business Improvement Area
item.media:description: The beaver statue honoring the United States in Toronto. One of dozens of statues of Canada’s national animal, the beaver was destroyed.

item.title: Aid Workers in Venezuela Fear Disease Outbreaks After Twin Quakes
item.description: Health care workers worry that illnesses like cholera could spread in areas where clean water was already in short supply, and last week’s earthquakes destroyed water systems.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.category: International Committee of the Red Cross
item.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
item.media:description: Collapsed buildings in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Monday. The death toll is above 1,900.

item.title: Trump Officials Sideline Machado, Venezuela’s Opposition Leader, Over Earthquake Response
item.description: U.S. officials called a bid by María Corina Machado, the Nobel Peace Prize winner, to return to earthquake-battered Venezuela a “political stunt” that has distracted from recovery efforts.
item.dc:creator: Anatoly Kurmanaev and Tyler Pager
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: A group of civilian volunteers search for earthquake survivors in Caribe, Venezuela, on Saturday.

item.title: China’s Plan to Save Jobs From A.I.
item.description: As it embeds the technology in every industry, China is thinking about how to keep humans employed.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:credit: Qilai Shen for The New York Times

item.title: The Two Mexico Cities Fighting for Claim Over Soccer’s Origin
item.description: Mineral del Monte, a small former mining town that became a tourist destination partly because of its Cornish influence, claims that soccer in Mexico started there. Well, so does the nearby city of Pachuca.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner
item.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tourists wearing Mexican national team jerseys in the center of Mineral del Monte, Mexico on Wednesday.

item.title: Venezuela’s Maduro Accused in Lawsuit of Authorizing Police Killings
item.description: Five families whose loved ones were killed during a deadly police campaign have sued the former Venezuelan leader in U.S. court in Brooklyn.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: A plaintiff photographed to disguise her identity. The lawsuit was filed using aliases to protect the families.

item.title: The 1968 Pact on Shipping Routes in the Strait of Hormuz That Iran Is Rejecting
item.description: For decades, a maritime agreement negotiated by Iran and Oman has governed the main pathways through the strait.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: A vessel in the Strait of Hormuz. A 1968 agreement was largely a technical solution to prevent collisions between supertankers there.

item.title: Putin Faces Increased Pressure as Moscow Is Again Attacked by Drones
item.description: The Russian authorities said 419 drones were shot down across Russia, including in the capital, and in Crimea.
item.dc:creator: Paul Sonne, Ivan Nechepurenko and John Yoon
item.media:credit: Andrei Vorobyov, via Telegram, via Reuters
item.media:description: A photograph posted on the Moscow region’s official social media channel on Tuesday, said to show a damaged private house in the town of Yegoryevsk.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands, Aaron Boxerman and Hari Raj

item.title: In Peru’s Presidential Election, Keiko Fujimori Gives the Right in Latin America Another Win
item.description: Keiko Fujimori, a daughter of the former strongman Alberto Fujimori, returns her family’s movement to power, but with a narrow victory in a divided country.
item.dc:creator: Mitra Taj and Genevieve Glatsky
item.media:credit: Anthony Nino De Guzman/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Keiko Fujimori waving to supporters during her closing campaign rally in Peru’s capital, Lima, in June. She has become the first woman to hold Peru’s highest office.

item.title: What the U.S. Owes Venezuela
item.description: Expectations are rising over the role the Trump administration could play in helping after two devastating quakes.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Volunteers in Caracas loaded donations and supplies to deliver to earthquake survivors on Friday.

item.title: Abdul Ahad Momand, Only Afghan to Fly in Space, Is Dead
item.description: A pilot in the Afghan Air Force, he was recruited for the Soviet space program. His 1988 flight took place as the Soviets were fighting a war in his country.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir

item.title: Efforts to Rescue Venezuela Quake Victims Grow More Desperate
item.description: The death toll has risen to over 1,700, which is likely an undercount.
item.dc:creator: Isayen Herrera, Fabiola Ferrero, Luis Ferré-Sadurní and Leo Sands
item.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
item.media:description: At La Guaira port, the authorities have set up an improvised morgue for relatives to recognize the bodies of the earthquake’s victims in Venezuela, on Monday.

item.title: Wimbledon Is One Place That Won’t Screen England in the World Cup
item.description: The tennis tournament, a bastion of sometimes stuffy rules, has no plans to broadcast World Cup matches on Henman Hill, but restrictions on cellphone use have been eased.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather
item.media:credit: Glyn Kirk/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Spectators watched Wimbledon action on Henman Hill on the grounds of the All England Lawn Tennis and Croquet Club last year. Those screens will not carry World Cup matches, the club’s chief executive said.

item.title: At Funerals, Venezuela’s Wounded Families and Friends Unite in Grief
item.description: Venezuelans began to bury some of the more than 1,700 victims of last week’s earthquakes, sharing tales of unimaginable loss.
item.dc:creator: Tibisay Romero, Fabiola Ferrero and Luis Ferré-Sadurní
item.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
item.media:description: Relatives and friends mourn at a funeral in Caracas, Venezuela, on Sunday.

item.title: Scale of Venezuela Earthquakes Death Toll Could Take Weeks to Emerge
item.description: Experts pointed to several indications that the twin earthquakes were particularly lethal, including the number of people reported missing and the extent of building damage.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rescue efforts have continued for days since the devastating earthquakes in Venezuela.

item.title: Les Mills, Olympian Who Founded Fitness Chain, Dies at 91
item.description: A New Zealand Olympian, he started a small gym in Auckland that grew into a worldwide chain.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather
item.media:credit: Jason Oxenham/The New Zealand Herald, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Les Mills in 2018. “He saw the gym as a place where people could come and find refuge,” his son, Phillip, said.

item.title: 6 Are Killed During a Rare Mass Shooting in Germany
item.description: The authorities said a 45-year-old man opened fire at a youth facility after a quarrel about the custody of his baby daughter. The dead were all facility employees.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley and Christopher F. Schuetze
item.media:credit: Ibrahim Ot/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The scene where five people were killed on Monday in a shooting in Stade, in northern Germany. A sixth victim died at a hospital.

item.title: Andy Burnham, the UK’s Likely Next Prime Minister, Promises Shift of Power Out of London
item.description: Andy Burnham, who looks set to become prime minister next month, said he would set up a new operation in Manchester called “No. 10 North” to give more funding and control to local leaders.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:credit: Toby Shepheard/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Andy Burnham, center, arrives to deliver a speech in Manchester, northern England, on Monday. He is expected to succeed Keir Starmer as prime minister from the Labour Party.

item.title: France Recorded 1,000 Excess Deaths During Heat Wave, Officials Say
item.description: First estimates published by the national health agency listed hundreds more deaths per day compared with the daily death rate in previous months.
item.dc:creator: Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:credit: Tom Nicholson/Reuters
item.media:description: Officials recovering the body of someone who drowned while swimming in a canal on Friday as Paris baked under a heat wave.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang, Isayen Herrera, Genevieve Glatsky, Fabiola Ferrero, Julie Turkewitz and Sheyla Urdaneta

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Shirin Hakim, Adam Rasgon, Natan Odenheimer, Elian Peltier and Aaron Boxerman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Whatever You Do in Russia, Don’t Talk About the War
item.description: As Ukraine brings the war home to Russia, officials hesitate to designate shelters and blast sirens, downplaying the conflict’s consequences with euphemisms.
item.dc:creator: Valerie Hopkins
item.media:credit: Igor Ivanko/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Manezhnaya Square, in Moscow, on Tuesday.

item.title: For These Teens in Mexico, Soccer Is Life. Now, the Cartels Want In.
item.description: In Celaya, Mexico, local cartels have turned soccer fields into bloody battlefields as they have co-opted a local league for teenagers as their latest business endeavor.
item.dc:creator: Maria Abi-Habib and Alejandro Cegarra
item.media:credit: Alejandro Cegarra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Toros de Celaya players during a practice session in Celaya, Mexico.

item.title: How Cartel Violence Is Infiltrating Local Soccer
item.description: In Salamanca, Mexico, soccer matches are being plagued by violence and deaths in multiple shootings. Our investigative correspondent Maria Abi-Habib reports from the town.
item.dc:creator: Maria Abi-Habib, Leila Medina, Thomas Vollkommer, Guillermo Alvarez and Joey Sendaydiego

item.title: In Japan’s ‘Little Brazil,’ a World Cup Showdown Tests Loyalties
item.description: More than 200,000 Brazilians live in Japan, after more than a century of migration between the countries. Many are of Japanese descent, so which team are they cheering on?
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández
item.media:credit: Noriko Hayashi for The New York Times

item.title: At a Caracas Morgue, Families and Officials Try to Identify More Than 100 Victims
item.description: Identifying victims has proved to be difficult because many bodies were badly crushed beneath collapsed buildings.
item.dc:creator: Isayen Herrera and Genevieve Glatsky
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: A flyer of a missing family lays outside of the morgue of the Dr. José María Vargas Hospital on Friday.

item.title: Europe’s Heat Politics
item.description: As the continent swelters, some right-wing populists are talking up their support for air-conditioning.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:credit: Paul Reid/Getty Images
item.media:description: Kemi Badenoch, the leader of Britain’s Conservative Party, visited an oil rig in Aberdeen, Scotland, in March.

item.title: In Venezuela, a Community Comes Together to Search for Earthquake Survivors
item.description: Volunteers in a middle-class neighborhood in Caracas used drills, picks and hammers to break through concrete, trying to find anyone in need of rescue.
item.dc:creator: Fabiola Ferrero and Julie Turkewitz
item.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rescue workers at the Residencia Rita apartment building in Caracas, Venezuela, on Saturday.

item.title: Search teams rescued 33 people from the rubble on Saturday.
item.dc:creator: Genevieve Glatsky
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Survivors are still being rescued after more than three days since the earthquakes.

item.title: Iran Risks Peace Talks With U.S. to Maintain Leverage Over Strait
item.description: Iran sees its control over the Strait of Hormuz as critical leverage in peace talks with the United States. It seems willing to risk the cease-fire to maintain that power.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Boats anchored off Oman’s northern Musandam Peninsula near the Strait of Hormuz on Saturday.

item.title: Deep Under the Rubble, Rescuers Find an 11-Year-Old Boy Alive
item.description: A Colombian rescue team worked for six hours to recover the child, Moises, from under nearly 10 feet of rubble in La Guaira. His rescue was captured on video.
item.dc:creator: Anushka Patil
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times

item.title: Helicopter Crash Near Major Saudi Oil Refinery Kills 14 People
item.description: The incident occurred near the Ras Tanura facility in the east of the country, a key export hub for oil transported through the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim
item.media:credit: Ahmed Jadallah/Reuters
item.media:description: Parts of the Ras Tanura oil refinery were closed in March after a fire following an Iranian drone attack.

item.title: Skydiving Plane Crashes in France, Killing All 11 People Aboard
item.description: Five instructors, five students and a pilot died when the plane crashed on Sunday morning in a field near the northeastern city of Nancy.
item.dc:creator: Ali Watkins and Leo Sands
item.media:credit: Christian Hartmann/Reuters
item.media:description: Those aboard the plane that crashed had been scheduled to take part in tandem skydiving lessons, the mayor of Nancy told local news media.

item.title: Dozens of Dogs Join Search for Venezuela Earthquake Survivors
item.description: Specially trained dogs can pick up the scent of survivors beneath mounds of collapsed debris, indicating where clearance teams should begin digging.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.media:credit: Juan Barreto/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A canine rescue unit from the Mexican army searching a damaged building in Caraballeda, Venezuela, on Friday.

item.title: Heat Wave Starts to Break in Western Europe, as Central Europe Begins to Broil
item.description: After days of record temperatures in Western Europe, the extreme heat is easing there. Now, Central Europe is facing days of dangerous swelter.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Isayen Herrera, María Victoria Fermín, Julie Turkewitz, Genevieve Glatsky, Fabiola Ferrero and Yan Zhuang

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman, Euan Ward, John Ismay, Tyler Pager and Ephrat Livni
